skillName: Round integers to the nearest 10, 100 or 1000
misconceptionSummary: Students do not understand why 5 rounds up — they think it should round to the nearest even.

visuals.ruleText: Rounding finds the nearest multiple of 10, 100 or 1000
visuals.subText: Think: which multiple is this number closest to?

steps.narration: Rounding is not a rule to memorise — it is finding the nearest landmark. Rounding four hundred and sixty to the nearest hundred asks: is it closer to four hundred or to five hundred?
Four sixty-three sits between four hundred and five hundred. The midpoint is four hundred and fifty. Since four sixty-three is above the midpoint, it is closer to five hundred.

lines.text: Midpoint of any range always rounds up
Convention: halfway goes to the larger value

steps.narration: When a number sits exactly on the midpoint — like four hundred and fifty — convention says round up to the larger multiple. This is consistent and agreed upon, even though both multiples are equally close.
Four hundred and sixty-three rounds to five hundred. It is past the midpoint, so the upper hundred is the nearest multiple.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: thinking the midpoint (e.g. 450) should round down because it is 'not yet past' the upper value. By convention, halfway always rounds up to the larger multiple.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Rounding finds the nearest multiple. At the exact midpoint, the rule is to round up — this is a convention everyone agrees to use.
